question: [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: IBuyCallback.onBuy / take] Can an unprivileged taker, acting as the sell-side of a buy offer with dust-sized units, cause units.mulDivDown(buyerPrice, WAD) == units.mulDivDown(sellerPrice, WAD) (both round to the same integer) so that claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += 0 while the onBuy callback receives buyerAssets == sellerAssets, under the precondition that units * settlementFee(id, timeToMaturity) < WAD, by calling take() with units chosen so that units * (buyerPrice - sellerPrice) < WAD, violating the invariant that every take must collect a non-zero settlement fee when settlementFee > 0, causing scoped impact: systematic fee leakage via zero-fee dust trades? Proof idea: fuzz units in [1, WAD / settlementFee - 1] for a buy offer at any tick with non-zero settlementFeeCbp, assert claimableSettlementFee delta == 0 and buyerAssets == sellerAssets.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: IBuyCallback.onBuy / take] Can an unprivileged taker, acting as the sell-side of a sell offer with dust-sized units, cause units.mulDivUp(buyerPrice, WAD) == units.mulDivUp(sellerPrice, WAD) (both round up to the same integer) so that claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += 0 while the onBuy callback is not called and the onSell callback receives sellerAssets == buyerAssets, under the precondition that units * settlementFee(id, timeToMaturity) < WAD and offer.buy == false, by calling take() with units chosen so that units * (buyerPrice - sellerPrice) < WAD, violating the invariant that every take must collect a non-zero settlement fee when settlementFee > 0, causing scoped impact: systematic fee leakage via zero-fee dust trades on sell offers? Proof idea: fuzz units in [1, WAD / settlementFee - 1] for a sell offer, assert claimableSettlementFee delta == 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: IBuyCallback.onBuy / take] Can an unprivileged maker deploying a buy offer with a callback contract observe that the onBuy callback receives buyerPendingFeeIncrease = buyerCreditIncrease.mulDivDown(continuousFee * timeToMaturity, WAD) which rounds down, so the callback underestimates the true continuous fee obligation by up to WAD-1 units, under the precondition that continuousFee > 0 and timeToMaturity > 0 and buyerCreditIncrease > 0, by calling take() and inspecting the pendingFeeIncrease parameter in onBuy, violating the invariant that the fee value passed to the callback must accurately reflect the fee charged to the buyer's position, causing scoped impact: callback-based integrations systematically underestimate continuous fee cost, leading to value leakage over many trades? Proof idea: unit test with buyerCreditIncrease = WAD - 1, continuousFee = 1, timeToMaturity = 1, assert pendingFeeIncrease == 0 while position.pendingFee increases by 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: IBuyCallback.onBuy / take] Can an unprivileged taker time a take() call to block.timestamp == offer.market.maturity so that timeToMaturity = zeroFloorSub(maturity, block.timestamp) = 0, causing buyerPendingFeeIncrease = buyerCreditIncrease.mulDivDown(continuousFee * 0, WAD) = 0, and the onBuy callback receives pendingFeeIncrease = 0 even though the buyer acquires new credit, under the precondition that block.timestamp == market.maturity and continuousFee > 0 and buyerCreditIncrease > 0, by warping to exactly maturity and calling take(), violating the invariant that credit acquired at maturity should still carry its full continuous fee obligation, causing scoped impact: zero continuous fee for credit acquired at the maturity boundary, extractable by timing? Proof idea: warp to maturity, take buy offer, assert position[id][buyer].pendingFee == 0 and credit > 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: IBuyCallback.onBuy / take] Can an unprivileged maker with a buy offer callback observe that onBuy is called BEFORE SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) and BEFORE SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets), so the callback sees fully updated credit/debt/consumed state but no tokens have moved yet, under the precondition that buyerCallback != address(0) and offer.buy == true, by deploying a callback that reads position[id][buyer].credit and marketState[id].withdrawable during onBuy, violating the invariant that callbacks must not observe partial state where accounting is updated but token balances are not, causing scoped impact: callback can observe and act on pre-transfer state to extract value via reentrancy into withdraw or repay? Proof idea: deploy callback that calls withdraw() during onBuy, assert it can drain withdrawable before fee transfer settles.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: ISellCallback.onSell / take] Can an unprivileged maker with a sell offer callback observe that onSell is called AFTER SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets) but the sellerAssets value passed to onSell was computed as units.mulDivUp(sellerPrice, WAD) (rounded up for sell offers), so the callback receives a sellerAssets value that is 1 wei higher than the floor of the true price, under the precondition that offer.buy == false and units * sellerPrice % WAD != 0, by deploying a sell callback that uses sellerAssets to compute expected token balance, violating the invariant that sellerAssets passed to onSell must equal the exact amount transferred to receiver, causing scoped impact: callback accounting desync if callback assumes floor rounding for sellerAssets? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: ISellCallback.onSell / take] Can an unprivileged taker observe that sellerPendingFeeDecrease passed to onSell is computed as sellerPos.pendingFee.mulDivUp(sellerCreditDecrease, sellerPos.credit) (rounds up), so the seller's pendingFee is decremented by more than the proportional share, under the precondition that sellerPos.credit > 0 and sellerCreditDecrease < sellerPos.credit and sellerPos.pendingFee * sellerCreditDecrease % sellerPos.credit != 0, by calling take() on a sell offer where the seller has existing credit and partial credit is consumed, violating the invariant that pendingFee decrements must be exactly proportional to credit decrements, causing scoped impact: systematic over-deduction of seller's pendingFee, reducing the continuous fee the protocol collects from the seller? Proof idea: fuzz sellerPos.credit, sellerPos.pendingFee, sellerCreditDecrease, assert pendingFee decrease > pendingFee * sellerCreditDecrease / sellerPos.credit.

[File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: ILiquidateCallback.onLiquidate / liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator observe that repaidUnits passed to onLiquidate is computed via double mulDivUp (seizedAssets.mulDivUp(liquidatedCollatPrice, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif)) when seizedAssets > 0, causing the liquidator to pay up to 2 wei more than the true price, under the precondition that seizedAssets > 0 and liquidatedCollatPrice % ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE != 0 and WAD % lif != 0, by calling liquidate() with seizedAssets input and a callback that uses repaidUnits to compute token approval, violating the invariant that repaidUnits must equal the exact amount pulled from the liquidator, causing scoped impact: callback-based liquidators systematically overpay by up to 2 wei per liquidation due to double rounding? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: ILiquidateCallback.onLiquidate / liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator observe that seizedAssets passed to onLiquidate is computed via double mulDivDown (repaidUnits.mulDivDown(lif, WAD).mulDivDown(ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, liquidatedCollatPrice)) when repaidUnits > 0, causing the liquidator to receive up to 2 wei less collateral than the true price, under the precondition that repaidUnits > 0 and repaidUnits * lif % WAD != 0 and (repaidUnits * lif / WAD) * ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE % liquidatedCollatPrice != 0, by calling liquidate() with repaidUnits input and a callback that uses seizedAssets to compute expected collateral receipt, violating the invariant that seizedAssets must equal the exact amount transferred to receiver, causing scoped impact: callback-based liquidators systematically receive less collateral than expected due to double rounding? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: ILiquidateCallback.onLiquidate / liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator exploit the post-maturity lif computation lif = min(_maxLif, WAD + (_maxLif - WAD) * (block.timestamp - market.maturity) / TIME_TO_MAX_LIF) which uses raw integer division (not mulDiv), causing lif to be truncated down by up to TIME_TO_MAX_LIF - 1 = 899 units, so that repaidUnits = seizedAssets.mulDivUp(liquidatedCollatPrice, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif) is slightly overestimated, under the precondition that postMaturityMode == true and block.timestamp - market.maturity < TIME_TO_MAX_LIF and (_maxLif - WAD) * (block.timestamp - market.maturity) % TIME_TO_MAX_LIF != 0, by calling liquidate() with seizedAssets input at a timestamp where lif truncation is maximal, violating the invariant that lif must accurately reflect the linear ramp, causing scoped impact: liquidator overpays repaidUnits due to truncated lif, creating extractable value for borrowers? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: ILiquidateCallback.onLiquidate / liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator observe that the badDebt value passed to onLiquidate is computed as the excess of _position.debt over the sum of collateral.mulDivUp(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, maxLif) for all activated collaterals, where mulDivUp rounds up the collateral value, causing badDebt to be underestimated (collateral value is rounded up, reducing the apparent bad debt), under the precondition that the borrower has multiple activated collaterals and each collateral's price * amount % ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE != 0, by calling liquidate() on a multi-collateral borrower with near-zero bad debt, violating the invariant that badDebt must equal the true excess debt not covered by collateral at maxLif, causing scoped impact: callback receives underestimated badDebt, leading to incorrect loss socialization accounting in callback-based integrations? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: ILiquidateCallback.onLiquidate / liquidate] Can an unprivileged liquidator observe that maxRepaid = (_position.debt - maxDebt).mulDivUp(WAD * WAD, WAD * WAD - lif * lltv) uses WAD * WAD = 1e36 as a fixed-point scale, and when lif * lltv is close to WAD * WAD (e.g., LLTV = 0.98e18 with cursor = 0.5, giving lif ≈ 1.0101e18 and lif * lltv ≈ 0.9899e36), the denominator WAD * WAD - lif * lltv ≈ 0.0101e36 is small, causing mulDivUp to round up significantly, so maxRepaid is overestimated by up to (WAD * WAD - lif * lltv - 1) / (WAD * WAD - lif * lltv) ≈ 1 unit, under the precondition that LLTV is near WAD and the position is slightly unhealthy, by calling liquidate() with repaidUnits = maxRepaid + 1 and observing the RCF check, violating the invariant that the RCF must prevent liquidation beyond what is needed to restore health, causing scoped impact: RCF overestimation allows slightly more liquidation than intended, leaving position slightly healthy post-liquidation? [File: 'src/interfaces/ICallbacks.sol -> Scope: High Rounding, fixed-point math, mulDiv, fee, spread, or time-to-maturity error that creates extractable value or systematic value leakage'] [Function: IBuyCallback.onBuy / settlementFee] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the settlement fee interpolation formula (feeLower * (end - timeToMaturity) + feeUpper * (timeToMaturity - start)) / (end - start) which uses integer division (truncates down), causing the settlement fee to be underestimated by up to (end - start - 1) units, so that claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += buyerAssets - sellerAssets collects less than the true fee, under the precondition that feeLower != feeUpper and timeToMaturity is not at a breakpoint boundary, by calling take() at a timeToMaturity where the interpolation truncates maximally, violating the invariant that the protocol must collect the full settlement fee as defined by the piecewise linear schedule, causing scoped impact: systematic settlement fee undercollection of up to (end - start - 1) / WAD per unit traded?
